title: Acceleration Calculator – Compute Rate of Velocity Change
tagline: Calculate acceleration from initial velocity, final velocity, and time
intro: Enter initial velocity, final velocity, and elapsed time to compute acceleration using a = (v − v₀) / t. Supports multiple velocity and time units.

error.invalid: Please enter valid numbers for initial velocity, final velocity, and time.
error.zeroTime: Time must be greater than zero.

examples.heading: Acceleration Examples
examples.intro: These examples illustrate acceleration calculations in everyday and scientific scenarios.

items.input: v₀ = 0 mph, v = 60 mph, t = 3 s
items.note: A sports car accelerating from standstill to 60 mph in 3 seconds. This is approximately 0.91 g, near the grip limit of high-performance tyres.

items.input: v₀ = 0 m/s, v = 39.2 m/s, t = 4 s
items.note: An object in free fall (ignoring air resistance) reaches approximately 39.2 m/s after 4 seconds, equalling standard gravitational acceleration.

items.input: v₀ = 120 km/h, v = 80 km/h, t = 10 s
items.note: A train braking from 120 km/h to 80 km/h in 10 seconds. The negative value indicates deceleration (slowing down).

items.input: v₀ = 0 m/s, v = 1000 m/s, t = 8 s
items.note: A rocket launching from rest to 1000 m/s in 8 seconds. The 12.74 g acceleration is extreme and would be fatal for unprotected humans.

howto.heading: How to use the Acceleration Calculator
steps: Enter the initial velocity (v₀) — the speed of the object at the start of the time interval. Use 0 if the object begins from rest.
Enter the final velocity (v) — the speed of the object at the end of the time interval.
Select the velocity unit from the dropdown: m/s, km/h, mph, or ft/s. Both velocities must be in the same unit.
Enter the elapsed time (t) and select the time unit (seconds, minutes, or hours).
Click Calculate to see the acceleration in m/s², the equivalent number of g-forces, and the change in velocity.

items.q: What is the formula for acceleration?
items.a: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time: a = (v − v₀) / t, where v is the final velocity, v₀ is the initial velocity, and t is the elapsed time. This formula gives average acceleration over the time interval. If the final velocity is less than the initial velocity, the result is negative, indicating deceleration. The SI unit of acceleration is metres per second squared (m/s²).

One g equals the standard acceleration due to Earth's gravity, approximately 9.80665 m/s². A car accelerating at 1 g would gain about 35 km/h every second. Fighter pilots can experience 9 g in a turn, while humans typically lose consciousness above 4–6 g sustained. Roller coasters typically peak at 3–5 g for a fraction of a second during the most intense manoeuvres.

items.q: What is the difference between acceleration and deceleration?
items.a: Deceleration is simply negative acceleration — when velocity decreases over time, the acceleration value is negative. In physics, both are described by the same formula a = Δv/t; the sign indicates direction. In everyday language, 'deceleration' means slowing down, while 'acceleration' implies speeding up, but mathematically they are the same quantity with opposite signs.

items.q: Can acceleration be calculated from distance and time?
items.a: Yes. If an object starts from rest (v₀ = 0) and travels a distance d in time t under constant acceleration, then a = 2d / t². This comes from the kinematic equation d = ½at². If the initial velocity is not zero, the full equation is d = v₀t + ½at², which can be rearranged to a = 2(d − v₀t) / t². This calculator uses velocity and time, but the kinematics are all related through these equations.

items.q: Why is acceleration measured in m/s²?
items.a: Acceleration is measured in metres per second squared (m/s²) because it represents the change in velocity (m/s) per unit of time (s). So 1 m/s² means the velocity increases by 1 metre per second every second. The squared unit arises because you are dividing m/s by s, giving m/s/s, which is written as m/s². Other common units include ft/s², km/h/s, and the g-unit (≈ 9.81 m/s²).

Uniform acceleration means the rate of velocity change is constant throughout the motion — the acceleration does not vary with time. This is the scenario the formula a = (v − v₀) / t strictly applies to. Free fall near Earth's surface (ignoring air resistance) and vehicles under constant engine thrust approximate uniform acceleration. Non-uniform acceleration, where the rate changes over time, requires integration of the acceleration function over time.

about.heading: About the Acceleration Calculator
about.body:
Acceleration is one of the three fundamental kinematic quantities in classical mechanics, alongside displacement and velocity. It describes how quickly an object's velocity changes over time and is the bridge between kinematics (describing motion) and dynamics (explaining why objects move the way they do through Newton's second law, F = ma).

The definition of average acceleration is straightforward: a = (v − v₀) / t, where v is the final velocity, v₀ is the initial velocity, and t is the duration of the change. If the final velocity exceeds the initial velocity, acceleration is positive (speeding up). If the final velocity is smaller, acceleration is negative, which is commonly called deceleration. The SI unit of acceleration is metres per second squared (m/s²).

In everyday life, acceleration is experienced whenever a vehicle speeds up, brakes, or turns. A car manufacturer's "0 to 60 mph" claim directly reflects acceleration performance — dividing the velocity change by the time gives the average acceleration. The standard gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface, g ≈ 9.81 m/s², serves as a convenient reference: a car achieving 0–100 km/h in 4 seconds has an average acceleration of about 6.9 m/s², or roughly 0.7 g.

In engineering and physics, acceleration plays a central role in structural analysis (inertial forces depend on acceleration), vehicle safety (crash tests measure deceleration pulses), rocketry (thrust-to-weight ratio determines the achieved g-level), and biomechanics (human tissue tolerates only limited acceleration before injury). Accelerometers — the sensors in smartphones, fitness trackers, and inertial navigation systems — directly measure acceleration and integrate it over time to estimate velocity and position.

This Acceleration Calculator applies the kinematic formula a = Δv / t and supports multiple input units (m/s, km/h, mph, ft/s for velocity; seconds, minutes, hours for time) to accommodate real-world data in any system of measurement. The result is displayed in m/s² and also expressed as a multiple of g, providing immediate intuition about the magnitude of the acceleration relative to everyday gravitational experience.
